data.title: Watch live: Bondi, Noem, Gabbard attend NASS conference
data.description:
Attorney General Pam Bondi, Department of Homeland Security (DHS) Secretary Kristi Noem and Director of National Intelligence Tulsi Gabbard will attend the 2026 National Association of Secretaries of State winter meeting and give remarks on Friday.

The annual conference comes as the Trump administration is facing scrutiny over its immigration enforcement tactics throughout the U.S. The criticism was exacerbated earlier this month after two fatal shootings of Minneapolis residents involving DHS officials.

The Justice Department (DOJ) on Friday confirmed that several protesters and journalists, including former CNN anchor Don Lemon, were arrested for disturbing a church service in St. Paul, Minn. DOJ also unveiled a new tranche of files related to convicted sex offender Jeffrey Epstein.

Gabbard has made headlines in recent days after she was spotted at an FBI raid of the Fulton County, Ga., election office — which was central to President Trump’s 2020 election fraud claims. Democrats have questioned why she was on the scene.

The event is scheduled to begin at 3 p.m. EST.

Watch the live video above.

data.title: US lifts some sanctions on Venezuelan oil
data.description:
The U.S. is lifting some sanctions on Venezuelan oil, seeking to allow American firms to trade and sell more oil that is produced there.

The move comes as the administration has said it will sell Venezuelan oil following its capture of leader Nicolás Maduro.

Previously, two commodities traders, Trafigura and Vitol, were given permission to help facilitate the sales.

The Treasury Department’s Office of Foreign Assets Control this week issued a general license that says U.S. companies can conduct some transactions related to the exploration, refining, sale or transport of Venezuelan oil

While U.S. firm Chevron is already producing oil in Venezuela, previously sanctioned oil was also being produced by state-run oil firm Petróleos de Venezuela. 

After the U.S. captured and arrested Maduro, President Trump said the South American country would turn over between 30 million and 50 million barrels that the president himself would control.

Energy Secretary Chris Wright later said the U.S. would market more than just those barrels and would control oil coming out of Venezuela “indefinitely.” The Energy Department said the administration would work with “the world’s leading commodity marketers and key banks to execute and provide financial support for these crude oil and crude products sales.”

data.title: Mark Carney praises Trump Fed pick Kevin Warsh
data.description:
Canadian Prime Minister Mark Carney praised President Trump’s nomination of former Federal Reserve board member Kevin Warsh to serve as its next chair in a post Friday on social platform X. 

“Kevin Warsh is a fantastic choice to lead the world’s most important central bank at this crucial time,” Carney said in the post. 

Carney was previously the governor of the Bank of Canada and the Bank of England, each country’s respective version of the Fed chair.

Warsh’s term as a Fed governor from 2006 to 2011 overlapped with Carney’s time as head of Canada’s central bank from 2008 to 2013. And during Carney’s stint leading the Bank of England, Warsh was hired to review the transparency of its policy meetings.

Carney’s vote of approval comes during a tense time for relations between the U.S. and Canada, after Carney delivered a speech at the World Economic Forum in Davos, Switzerland, last week pushing back on Trump’s tariff agenda and backing Greenland’s sovereignty.

He reaffirmed the sentiments in his speech in comments to reporters Tuesday, referencing a phone call he had with Trump on Monday. 

“To be absolutely clear, and I said this to the president, I meant what I said in Davos,” Carney told reporters.

Trump responded to Carney by retracting his invitation for Canada to join the Board of Peace, which Trump formed to oversee the rebuilding of Gaza. Carney had expressed skepticism about the terms and scope of the board.

Since the speech, Trump has also threatened steep new tariffs on Canada if it entered a new trade pact with China, which Carney said it had no plans of doing, and on Thursday night he threatened to restrict Canadian airplane imports.  

Trump officials reportedly met with a far-right separatist group from the oil-rich province of Alberta, Canada — a meeting that British Columbia Premier David Eby said amounted to “treason,” in remarks Thursday.

“To go to a foreign country and to ask for assistance in breaking up Canada, there’s an old-fashioned word for that. And that word is treason,” Eby told reporters in Ottawa. 

The White House did not deny the meetings but said “no support or commitments were conveyed.”

Trump has repeatedly suggested Canada should become the “51st state,” an idea that Carney has dismissed outright.

data.title: What to know about the temporary government shutdown this weekend
data.description:
The government is likely to temporarily shut down this weekend after the Senate failed to pass a funding package on Thursday due to challenges from Democrats to a Department of Homeland Security (DHS) bill. 

Here’s what you need to know about the partial government shutdown. 

Why is the government temporarily shutting down? 

After federal agents in Minneapolis fatally shot and killed a second Minnesotan, Alex Pretti, last Saturday, Senate Democratic Leader Chuck Schumer (D-N.Y.) said he and other Democrats would not back the minibus funding package unless a bill that appropriated funds to DHS was removed. 

Senate Majority Leader John Thune (R-S.D.) initially resisted these changes, but after seven Republicans joined all Democrats in the Thursday vote, he agreed to split the DHS bill from the rest of the package. Thune switched his vote to “no” on Thursday for procedural reasons. 

Despite having an agreed-upon deal with input from the Trump administration, the Senate punted a vote late Thursday night due to a hold from Sen. Lindsey Graham (R-S.C.) over other matters. But optimism returned on Friday after the South Carolina senator vowed to release his hold. The upper chamber is now on track to pass the government funding package later on Friday, but an exact vote time has not been confirmed.

If the Senate approves the changes, the package will return to the House, which passed the original funding package last week.  

However, the House is currently in recess, so the vote will have to wait until the chamber comes back into session next week, meaning Congress will miss the 11:59 p.m. Friday deadline. That also means that funding for 78 percent of the federal government will lapse at midnight on Saturday.

What’s in the current package?

The minibus package covers 96 percent of government funding. 

Included in the original $1.2 trillion package was funding for the Departments of Defense, Labor, Health and Human Services, Education, Transportation, Housing and Urban Development and DHS.

The DHS bill included an additional $10 billion to Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE). ICE is already set to receive $76 billion over the next four years under the One Big Beautiful Bill Act. 

In the deal reached by the Senate on Thursday evening, the DHS bill will now be separately considered as a stopgap funding bill that would fund the agency at current levels through Feb. 13. The five other bills will be considered altogether. 

Will the public see any of the effects?

While a temporary partial shutdown over the weekend is likely, if the House approves the measure early next week, the impacts are expected to be limited. 

The agencies covered under the five bills in the spending package and DHS would temporarily close, starting at midnight on Friday. 

The Office of Budget Management (OMB) has been communicating with agencies this week about a likely partial shutdown, an OMB spokesperson told The Hill. The spokesperson said guidance will be sent out later on Friday and that agencies will begin notifying employees who are exempted from the closure. 

Weekend shutdowns are fairly common and would not come with the same impacts that occurred last fall, when the government shut down for a total of 43 days. Of 21 shutdowns in congressional history, nearly half have lasted three days or less. 

Last October’s shutdown was the longest in U.S. history and resulted in federal workers being furloughed and an economic dip. 

A report released by the Congressional Research Service (CRS) on Thursday estimated that around 670,000 federal workers were furloughed during the shutdown — around 0.4 percent of the federal government’s total employment numbers. 

When will the House act?

The House is expected to reconvene on Monday. No votes have been scheduled yet on the funding measure.

data.title: Catherine O'Hara, star of 'Home Alone' and 'Schitt's Creek,' dies at 71
data.description:
(NEXSTAR) – Renowned actress Catherine O’Hara has died at the age of 71.

The Canadian-born O’Hara died at her home in Los Angeles “following a brief illness,” according to a statement from her agency, CAA. Further details were not immediately available.

O’Hara is well known for a number of hit movies, including “Home Alone,” “Beetlejuice,” “The Nightmare Before Christmas” and “Best in Show,” along with the hit series “Schitt’s Creek.”

O’Hara’s time at the Second City in the 1970’s propelled her comedic career and led to the creation of the sketch comedy series “S.C.T.V.,” in which she starred alongside John Candy, Rick Moranis and Eugene Levy. The show would help launch the careers of other top Canadian comedians, like Martin Short and Andrea Martin.

After leaving the Second City, O’Hara would continue to collaborate with Levy for decades. Levy, who played O’Hara’s husband Johnny Rose on “Schitt’s Creek,” also costarred with her in “Waiting for Guffman,” “Best in Show,” “A Mighty Wind” and “For Your Consideration.”

O’Hara recalled preparing for her role as Moira Rose on Schitt’s Creek, telling Vulture:

“I was afraid I would lean on being a bitchy wife [with Moira] because I often did that. When in doubt, I played either insane or bitchy at Second City Theater. And I didn’t want to do that with Eugene, and I’m so happy either of us didn’t go the way of not loving each other. I think we’re a great loving couple in this.”

The small show created by Levy and his son Dan about a wealthy family forced to live in a tiny town would dominate the Emmys in its sixth and final season. It brought O’Hara, always a beloved figure, a new generation of fans and put her at the center of cultural attention.

The Toronto native won two Emmys, a Golden Globe, American and Canadian Comedy Awards and two SAG Awards during her career.

O’Hara’s dramatic turn in HBO’s “The Last of Us” earned her an Emmy nomination, as did her recent role as a Hollywood producer in “The Studio.”

She is survived by her husband, Bo Welch, and sons Matthew and Luke.

The Associated Press contributed to this report.

data.title: The Clintons have no (legal) defense. Will they seek a deal?
data.description:
The House Oversight and Government Reform Committee voted on Jan. 21 to hold Bill and Hillary Clinton in contempt for refusing to comply with subpoenas in the committee’s Jeffrey Epstein investigation. The resolution now heads to the full House. If it gets a floor vote and passes, Speaker Mike Johnson (R-La.) will certify the matter to the Justice Department for criminal prosecution.

The Department of Justice will have a hard time justifying a refusal to prosecute.

The Clintons aren’t executive branch officials claiming privilege. They’re private citizens who simply refused to show up. They have offered no viable legal defense. And thanks to the Steve Bannon and Peter Navarro prosecutions, the Department of Justice has already established an institutional precedent that it will prosecute contempt cases against high-profile political figures when the law is clear.

My bet is they make a deal. Unless someone powerful decides this whole thing needs to go away.

The Clintons were subpoenaed by a House committee conducting a legitimate legislative investigation. Both Clintons were ordered to appear for depositions. Neither showed up.

Their attorney sent letters arguing the subpoenas lack “legal merit” and comparing the investigation to McCarthyism. But those aren’t viable reasons to disregard subpoenas. And they aren’t sound legal defenses to a criminal contempt charge.

The contempt statute makes it a crime to willfully refuse to comply with a congressional subpoena. It’s a misdemeanor punishable by up to one year in prison and a fine of up to $100,000. The elements are straightforward: (1) a valid subpoena, (2) willful refusal to comply, (3) the refusal was without legal justification.

The Clintons check all three boxes.

They’re certainly in no better position than Bannon and Navarro. And, those prosecutions established something important: The Department of Justice as an institution won’t shy from prosecuting contempt of Congress against high-profile figures from the political party adverse to the president when the legal case is clear.

If the House refers the Clinton contempt case to DOJ, Attorney General Pam Bondi’s team will have to decide whether to prosecute for contempt. The law favors a prosecution. So does precedent.

On paper, this should be an easy call.

Given the above, it seems like a no brainer that the Clintons would make a deal. They know that a federal criminal prosecution is no joke, and neither is a stint in federal prison, even a minimum security camp. They probably wouldn’t want to put all their faith in the possibility that a jury in Washington, D.C. would nullify a solid criminal case. Logic says they cut a deal to testify.

The Clintons’ attorney has already offered sworn statements and proposed a closed-door interview between Bill Clinton and Oversight and Government Reform Committee Chairman James Comer (R-Ky.). The committee rejected that and demanded formal depositions. The committee wants transcribed testimony, under oath, that creates a permanent record. Testimony that can be released to the public. Testimony that might implicate other people.

And that’s where the graymail possibility comes in. What if the Clintons’ testimony actually would implicate others? What if they would testify about others in Epstein’s orbit?

That could make some very powerful people very uncomfortable.

Trump himself might not be enthusiastic about what they’d say in a deposition. He has already expressed concern about disseminating information that, in his view, unjustly implicates innocent people who had only passing contact with Epstein. He might not want the Clintons to testify. Same goes for other powerful people whose names the Clintons might drop.

Before any of this gets to DOJ, the House has to vote. The Oversight and Government Reform Committee voted to advance the contempt resolutions, with some Democratic support. But the full House is a different calculation.

Does the Speaker bring the resolution to the floor? Or will the House be “too busy” with other matters? Republicans hold just a two-seat majority in the full House, so member absences matter. And it’s not clear if the Democratic support from the committee will transfer to the floor.

If someone powerful wants this investigation to go away, killing the floor vote is the cleanest way to do it.

Here’s my read: The Clintons make a deal, though it’s more likely to involve just Bill Clinton testifying. He agrees to sit for a deposition with clear limits on scope and timing in exchange for the House not pursuing contempt against both him and Hillary. The committee gets some testimony, and everyone moves on.

This is the most likely path. Unless enough House members push hard for more extensive testimony or someone decides they don’t want any Clinton testimony. In which case the floor vote becomes the pressure point. A quiet word to the Speaker, a few members suddenly develop concerns about the precedent this sets, and the contempt resolution dies before it ever gets to the Department of Justice.

It’s a distinct possibility. Because we don’t know how problematic public testimony would be for the Clintons — or for others. Maybe this is much ado about nothing. Or maybe there are reasons beyond legal exposure that powerful people would prefer this investigation just fade away.

Either way, the legal reality is clear: if this gets to DOJ, the Clintons are in real trouble unless the White House intervenes. The question for now is whether the case gets that far.

Ross H. Garber is the founding principal at The Garber Group LLC.

data.title: Reid Hoffman argues Silicon Valley 'can't bend the knee to Trump' after fatal shootings
data.description:
LinkedIn co-founder Reid Hoffman argued Thursday that Silicon Valley “can’t bend the knee” to President Trump after a second American was killed by federal agents amid an immigration crackdown in Minneapolis.

Hoffman, a major Democratic donor, said in an op-ed in the San Francisco Standard that too many tech leaders have “divested themselves of the responsibility to speak out against the administration’s excesses.”

“January’s tragic events in Minneapolis should end that posture,” he wrote. “We leaders in tech and business have power — economic, social, platform power — and sitting on that power right now is not good business. It’s also not neutrality. It’s a choice.”

Tensions have been running high in Minneapolis since 37-year-old Alex Pretti was shot and killed by Border Patrol agents Saturday. His death came amid protests over the fatal shooting of 37-year-old Renee Good by an Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE) agent just weeks earlier.

“I’ve been encouraged in recent days that more Silicon Valley leaders have spoken out to condemn the Minneapolis killings and the policy choices that led to them,” Hoffman said. “It’s a good start to something America needs much more of right now.”

Several major tech leaders have voiced concerns in the wake of Pretti’s shooting. OpenAI CEO Sam Altman criticized ICE for “going too far” in a message to employees obtained by The New York Times.

In a post on X, Anthropic CEO Dario Amodei underscored the “importance of preserving democratic values and rights at home” amid the “horror” in Minnesota.

Apple CEO Tim Cook said in a note to employees obtained by Bloomberg that he was “heartbroken” over the events in Minneapolis and suggested it was “a time for deescalation.”

“We in Silicon Valley can’t bend the knee to Trump,” Hoffman added in Thursday’s op-ed. “We can’t shrink away and just hope the crisis will fade. We know now that hope without action is not a strategy — it’s an invitation for Trump to trample whatever he can see, including our own business and security interests.”

The tech industry has enjoyed a largely positive relationship with Trump in his second term, in a stark reversal from the often-contentious exchanges between Silicon Valley and the president in his first term.

Tech leaders have appeared keen to stay on Trump’s good side since he secured the presidency in 2024, visiting him at Mar-a-Lago, donating to his inaugural fund and sitting front-row at his inauguration.

They have frequently attended events at the White House over the past year and have found a close ally in the administration as they have sought to fend off a growing number of state laws regulating AI.

Both Altman and Cook notably withheld criticism of Trump in their messages this week. Altman called the president a “very strong leader” and said he hopes he “will rise to this moment and unite the country,” while Cook noted that he had a “good conversation” with Trump and said he appreciates “his openness to engaging on issues that matter to us all.”

data.title: Judge refuses to release DC pipe bomb suspect
data.description:
A federal judge has rejected a request to be released pending trial from the suspect accused of planting pipe bombs outside the Republican and Democratic national committees ahead of the Jan. 6, 2021, Capitol attack.  

U.S. District Judge Amir Ali, an appointee of former President Biden, ruled Thursday that Brian Cole Jr. must remain locked up while awaiting trial.  

The decision keeps intact a magistrate judge’s ruling that no conditions of release could reasonably keep the public safe from the danger allegedly posed by Cole, who has pleaded not guilty to two federal explosive-related charges.  

Cole’s attorneys pointed to his lack of criminal record and diagnoses for autism and obsessive-compulsive disorder as reasons to let him out of jail as the case progresses. 

They said at a hearing Wednesday that the idea that strict release conditions wouldn’t keep Cole in line is “entirely farcical based on the facts.”  

“The actual nature of the offense matters here,” Ali pushed back. 

Cole’s arrest last month came after what government officials called an “aha moment” led to a breakthrough in a nearly five-year investigation. They’ve said purchasing records, phone data and other evidence were used to identify him. 

In his ruling earlier this month, U.S. Magistrate Judge Matthew Sharbaugh said Cole’s apparent continued purchasing of materials used to make explosives through the summer of 2022 — on top of investigators finding those materials in his home and car upon his arrest — refuted the notion that the past alleged conduct amounted to “some anomalous event.”  

Cole’s attorneys have argued that he had a proclivity for “science experiments,” and that his experimenting with potassium chlorate — a component in explosives — amounted to efforts to create “rocket fuel.” His mother affirmed as much in a sworn declaration submitted to the court that suggested the experiments began earlier than prosecutors have said.  

But Assistant U.S. Attorney Charles Jones said the declaration prompted federal investigators to look further into Cole’s purchasing history, where he said they found that, in 2018, Cole bought beaker sets and the sulfur dust used in the pipe bombs. He said that “fins” and “cones” for a model rocket were not among Cole’s purchases.  

The prosecutor claimed it shows the pipe bomb incident was not isolated, but a “yearslong continuum of conduct.” Defense attorneys rejected the government’s assessment. 

Jones also said that the pipe bombs placed outside the Republican National Committee and Democratic National Committee on Jan. 5, 2021, had “all the components” of a functional explosive device.  

The devices did not detonate five years ago but an explosives examiner for the government was able to explain how the materials in the bombs could be “put…back together” to create a functional device, he said. 

Jones said that Cole told investigators he was “worried” the devices were volatile, allegedly putting them in his backpack one at a time over concern they’d explode on him.  

“He certainly believed that those devices were viable,” the prosecutor said. 

Cole’s next hearing is set for Feb. 27. A trial date has not yet been scheduled.  

If convicted, Cole faces a maximum sentence of 10 years in prison on the count of transporting an explosive device in interstate commerce, while the count of attempted malicious destruction by means of explosive materials carries a five-year minimum sentence and up to 20 years behind bars.

data.title: Queen's Brian May says he's 'thinking twice' about performing in US: 'America is a dangerous place'
data.description:
(NEXSTAR) – English musician Brian May, the lead guitar player for Queen, hinted that the band probably would not perform in the U.S. in the near future, if at all.

“America is a dangerous place at the moment, so you have to take that into account,” the 78-year-old rock legend told the Daily Mail.

“It’s very sad because I feel like Queen grew up in America and we love it, but it’s not what it was,” he continued. “Everyone is thinking twice about going there at the moment.”

Brian May performs at the Platinum Jubilee concert taking place in front of Buckingham Palace, London, Saturday June 4, 2022. (AP Photo/Alberto Pezzali, Pool)

The Daily Mail did not say what prompted May’s comments, nor if they were referencing the recent killings of Minneapolis residents Renee Good or Alex Pretti by federal immigration officials deployed to Minnesota. The musician, however, has previously suggested that he was not a fan of President Trump, and had called out Trump’s campaign for using “We Are the Champions” during the 2016 presidential primaries.

“I will make sure we take what steps we can to dissociate ourselves from Donald Trump’s unsavoury campaign,” May remarked on his official website, The Guardian reported at the time.

The guitarist last performed in the U.S. in 2025, appearing with “Beautiful Things” singer Benson Boone during the Coachella Valley Music and Arts Festival in Indio, Calif. Queen, with Adam Lambert on lead vocals, had also toured the U.S. in 2019 and 2023.

Speaking with Rolling Stone in 2025, May had also expressed a desire to perform a residency at The Sphere in Las Vegas, telling the outlet that Queen was taking part in “conversations” about it.

I’m very keen on the Sphere,” he told the magazine. “It’s got my mind working.”

May, meanwhile, said he doesn’t know the next time Queen will take the stage at all, let alone in the U.S.

“It’s an unknown,” he told the Daily Mail. “We’ll take it day by day.”

data.title: DOJ opens federal civil rights probe into the death of Alex Pretti, but holds back on Renee Good
data.description:
The Justice Department’s Civil Rights Division will join a variety of federal entities investigating the killing of Minneapolis nurse Alex Pretti, tapping the involvement of a segment of DOJ that reviews excessive force by police.

Deputy Attorney General Todd Blanche said the division would work alongside the FBI and a division of the Department of Homeland Security in reviewing the deadly shooting. 

DOJ has faced steep criticism from within the ranks of the department over how it has handled the killings of Pretti and Renee Good, who was also shot by an immigration officer this month.

Blanche, however, cautioned against elevating the role of the Civil Rights Division in the investigation.

“I don’t want the takeaway to be that there’s some massive civil rights investigation that’s happening. This is what I would describe as a standard investigation by the FBI when there’s circumstances like what we saw last Saturday, and that that investigation, to the extent it needs to involve lawyers at the Civil Rights Division, it will involve those,” Blanche said.

“An investigation…means talking to witnesses, it means looking at documentary evidence, sending subpoenas, if you have to, and the Department of Justice, Civil Rights Division has the best experts in the world at this. They’ve been doing it for decades.”

However, Blanche stopped short of saying whether the division would be involved in reviewing the shooting of Good, saying he was “not committing to anything with respect to that investigation.”

“It doesn’t mean that every time that there is a federal-officer related shooting that that’s something Civil Rights takes up. It depends on the circumstances.”

The Washington Post reported Thursday that federal prosecutors in Minneapolis were prepared to resign en masse over how both shootings have been handled. At least half a dozen prosecutors in the office resigned when asked not to probe Good’s death but instead begin investigating her partner.

The two officers that shot Pretti, a VA nurse, have been placed on administrative leave.

Homeland Security Secretary Kristi Noem said Thursday during an appearance on Fox that while her department is investigating, the FBI had taken the lead.

The bureau is also the lead investigative agency on the Good shooting, and has thus far refused to share any information with Minnesota officials.

“We will continue to follow the investigation that the FBI is leading and giving them all the information that they need to bring that to conclusion, and make sure that the American people know the truth of the situation and how we can go forward and continue to protect the American people,” Noem said while speaking with Sean Hannity.
